subsystem: KDE Ark archive-manager LibarchivePlugin. The single method LibarchivePlugin::extractionFlags() returns the bitmask of libarchive archive_write_disk (ARCHIVE_EXTRACT_*) option flags that the plugin passes to archive_write_disk_set_options() when it extracts entries from a user-supplied archive to disk. It governs the security posture of the on-disk extraction step.
provenance: Identified from the class name LibarchivePlugin and the method name extractionFlags(), which are the Ark (kde/ark) libarchive back-end, together with the libarchive public constants ARCHIVE_EXTRACT_TIME, ARCHIVE_EXTRACT_SECURE_NODOTDOT and ARCHIVE_EXTRACT_SECURE_SYMLINKS defined in archive.h. The leading banner comment '/* sample: primevul_train_2442_fixed */' and the filename train_0191_cwe61.cpp mark this as the FIXED revision of a CWE-61 (UNIX symlink following) sample; the fix is the addition of the two ARCHIVE_EXTRACT_SECURE_* bits to the returned mask.
trust_boundary: The archive being extracted is fully attacker-controlled: a user opens or downloads a .tar/.zip/.7z-style archive from an untrusted source and asks Ark to extract it. Every entry pathname, entry type (regular file vs symlink vs directory), and symlink target inside the archive is hostile input. The flags returned here are the ONLY policy that constrains how those hostile pathnames and symlink entries are materialised on the victim's filesystem, so this bitmask is the security gate between the untrusted archive stream and the user's home directory.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Declares a const member function of LibarchivePlugin returning an int. In the Ark libarchive back-end this accessor centralises the ARCHIVE_EXTRACT_* option bitmask that is later handed to libarchive's disk writer (archive_write_disk_set_options). Because it is const and takes no arguments, the returned policy is static and identical for every archive and every entry: there is no per-entry or per-source hardening decision here, so whatever bits are OR'd together below are the complete, unconditional extraction policy for the plugin.

Begins the returned bitmask with ARCHIVE_EXTRACT_TIME. This libarchive flag tells archive_write_disk to restore each entry's modification timestamp from the archive metadata onto the extracted file. It is a fidelity/usability option, not a security control: it copies attacker-supplied mtime values onto disk but does not itself decide file placement, permissions, or link handling. It is the benign, non-security portion of the mask.

OR's in ARCHIVE_EXTRACT_SECURE_NODOTDOT. This is a libarchive security flag that makes the disk writer refuse to extract any entry whose pathname contains a '..' component. It defeats the classic Zip-Slip / tar path-traversal primitive where an entry named like '../../.bashrc' would escape the chosen destination directory and overwrite files elsewhere. This is one of the two bits that constitute the CWE-61/CWE-22 fix in this sample.

OR's in ARCHIVE_EXTRACT_SECURE_SYMLINKS and terminates the expression. This libarchive security flag makes the writer refuse to follow a symbolic link when placing a subsequent entry: if any leading path component of an entry resolves through a symlink that was itself extracted from the archive (or already exists as a symlink), extraction of that entry is rejected rather than writing through the link. This is the direct mitigation for CWE-61 (UNIX symbolic-link following): without it, an archive can contain a symlink entry 'evil -> /home/victim/.ssh' followed by a regular-file entry 'evil/authorized_keys', and the second write would land outside the destination. This bit is the core of the 'fixed' sample.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The mask omits ARCHIVE_EXTRACT_SECURE_NOABSOLUTEPATHS. SECURE_NODOTDOT blocks '..' traversal and SECURE_SYMLINKS blocks link following, but neither refuses an entry whose pathname is absolute (a leading '/'). libarchive's default for an absolute path is to STRIP the leading slash and extract relative to the destination, which is usually safe, but relying on that implicit default rather than explicitly setting SECURE_NOABSOLUTEPATHS means the absolute-path policy is not asserted here and could regress if the writer options change. An attacker who wants entries interpreted as absolute paths is only stopped by libarchive's default behaviour, not by an explicit flag in this policy. The invariant is that absolute-path entries must never be written outside the chosen destination; confirm this holds via SECURE_NOABSOLUTEPATHS or the writer's default and do not assume it from this mask alone.

This function only NAMES the policy; the entire security benefit is latent and lives in the (unshown) implementation that must actually pass this return value into archive_write_disk_set_options() for every archive_write_disk handle used during extraction. If any extraction path constructs a writer without applying extractionFlags(), or applies it before the handle is created, or a preview/test-extract code path uses a different mask, the SECURE_* bits are silently absent and the CWE-61 symlink-following and path-traversal exposure returns in full. The invariant is that no extraction code path may write entries to disk without this exact mask (or a stricter one) applied to its libarchive writer.

SECURE_SYMLINKS protects against symlinks that are components of a path the writer creates, but it does not sanitise the TARGET of a symlink entry that is itself extracted. A symlink entry pointing at an absolute or '..'-laden target (e.g. 'link -> ../../../../etc/passwd') can still be written to disk as a dangling/dangerous symlink; the danger is only realised when some later entry or later operation traverses it. SECURE_SYMLINKS covers the later-traversal-during-extraction case, but any post-extraction consumer that follows the extracted symlink (Ark's own preview/open, or the user) is still exposed. The invariant is that extracted symlink targets are treated as untrusted by every downstream consumer, which this flag alone does not guarantee.

summary: This is the FIXED revision of a CWE-61 symlink-following sample: LibarchivePlugin::extractionFlags() now returns ARCHIVE_EXTRACT_SECURE_NODOTDOT | ARCHIVE_EXTRACT_SECURE_SYMLINKS alongside ARCHIVE_EXTRACT_TIME, which correctly closes the tar/zip path-traversal and symlink-following primitives at the extraction gate. Residual, low-severity notes only: the mask does not explicitly assert ARCHIVE_EXTRACT_SECURE_NOABSOLUTEPATHS (relies on libarchive's default slash-stripping), and the whole benefit is latent on the unshown code actually applying this mask to every archive_write_disk handle.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Static, unconditional policy accessor whose return value is the sole extraction-security gate; latent finding that it must actually be applied to every writer.
Fidelity flag restoring entry mtime; carries no path-placement, permission, or link-following security contract.
Present and correct — this is part of the fix; it blocks '..' path traversal, so it is a control that IS set, not a gap.
Core CWE-61 mitigation and it IS set here, but it does not sanitise extracted symlink TARGETS for downstream consumers — worth a residual note.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: Absence of ARCHIVE_EXTRACT_SECURE_NOABSOLUTEPATHS in the returned mask (lines 4-6)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Absolute-path (leading '/') entries are governed only by libarchive's implicit default, not by an explicit flag in this policy.

findings.title: Extraction hardening is latent: the SECURE_* mask only helps if the impl applies it to every archive_write_disk handle
findings.bug_class: Symlink following / path traversal on archive extraction (latent — depends on unshown caller)
findings.invariant: Every code path that writes archive entries to disk must create its libarchive archive_write_disk handle and call archive_write_disk_set_options() with exactly this mask (or a stricter one) BEFORE any header is written; no extraction, preview, or test-extract path may use a writer without these SECURE_* bits.
findings.failure_mode: If any extraction path (including preview/open-inner-file, or a test/dry-run path) constructs a disk writer without applying extractionFlags(), the SECURE_NODOTDOT and SECURE_SYMLINKS protections are silently absent for that path and a crafted archive containing a symlink entry followed by a file written through it (e.g. 'link -> /home/user/.ssh' then 'link/authorized_keys'), or a '..' traversal entry, escapes the destination and overwrites arbitrary user files — the original CWE-61 impact returns.
findings.what_to_confirm: Grep the plugin for archive_write_disk_set_options / archive_write_disk_set_standard_lookup and confirm extractionFlags() is the argument on every writer, applied before writing entries, on all extraction and preview code paths.

findings.title: Absolute-path entries not explicitly refused (ARCHIVE_EXTRACT_SECURE_NOABSOLUTEPATHS missing)
findings.bug_class: Path traversal via absolute pathname (reliance on implicit default)
findings.invariant: An archive entry whose name is absolute (leading '/') must never be materialised outside the user-chosen destination directory; the policy should assert this explicitly rather than depend on libarchive's default of stripping the leading slash.
findings.failure_mode: With SECURE_NODOTDOT and SECURE_SYMLINKS but not SECURE_NOABSOLUTEPATHS, absolute-path entries are handled by libarchive's default (strip leading '/', extract relatively). That default is generally safe today, but the policy does not assert it, so an option or version change in how the writer is configured could allow absolute-path writes; SECURE_NOABSOLUTEPATHS would make the writer refuse such entries outright instead of relying on stripping behaviour.
findings.what_to_confirm: Decide whether Ark should refuse absolute-path entries (add ARCHIVE_EXTRACT_SECURE_NOABSOLUTEPATHS) versus silently rebasing them, and confirm the chosen behaviour is intended for all supported archive formats.

findings.title: Extracted symlink targets remain untrusted for post-extraction consumers
findings.bug_class: Dangerous symlink target written to disk (residual)
findings.invariant: Any Ark code that opens, previews, or otherwise follows a just-extracted path must treat symlink targets from the archive as hostile; SECURE_SYMLINKS only prevents the writer from writing THROUGH a link during extraction, not from creating a symlink entry with an absolute/escaping target.
findings.failure_mode: A crafted symlink entry (e.g. 'preview.txt -> /etc/shadow') is extracted as-is; SECURE_SYMLINKS does not sanitise its target. If Ark's preview/open-with feature or the user subsequently follows that link, it dereferences an out-of-tree target, leading to information disclosure or writing through the link.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check whether Ark resolves/opens extracted paths (preview, open-with, drag-out) and whether it canonicalises and confines them to the extraction directory before following.

audit_checklist: Confirm archive_write_disk_set_options() is called with extractionFlags() on EVERY writer, before any entry header is written, across extract/preview/test paths.
Decide and enforce absolute-path policy: add ARCHIVE_EXTRACT_SECURE_NOABSOLUTEPATHS or confirm libarchive's default slash-stripping is acceptable for all formats.
Verify no alternate extraction path (7z/rar/other back-ends, or a fast/preview path) uses a weaker or empty flag mask.
Ensure extracted symlink targets are treated as untrusted by any preview/open/drag-out consumer and confined to the destination directory.
Confirm the destination directory itself cannot be a pre-existing attacker-controlled symlink before extraction begins.
Regression-test with a corpus of malicious archives (Zip-Slip '..' entries, symlink-then-write pairs, absolute-path entries) to prove the mask blocks them end to end.
